topics.content: The Marathas were a group of people native to the Deccan plateau, specifically present-day Maharashtra. They were identified by their language, Marathi, which has a rich literary history dating back to the 12th century.
topics.additional_notes: The region was ruled by the Yadava dynasty (capital Devagiri) before the Khilji Sultanate overran it in the 14th century.

topics.content: Between the 7th and 17th centuries, saints like Dnyaneshwar, Namdev, Tukaram, and Ramdas gained popularity. They translated texts like the Upanishads and Bhagavad Gita into Marathi, making spiritual philosophy accessible to the masses.

examples.description: The Bhakti movement focused on social organization and political awareness, creating a solid cultural foundation that helped the Marathas organize into a political power.

notes.section_title: Foundation of Maratha Power: Shivaji Maharaj

topics.content: Born in 1630 to Shahji Bhonsle and Jijabai, Shivaji grew up in Pune under his mother's watchful eyes. He received values and education that shaped his vision of 'Swarajya' (a sovereign kingdom).
topics.additional_notes: At the age of 16, he began military campaigns by capturing neglected forts like Torna to consolidate his hold over the Pune region.

topics.content: To protect his people from larger enemies, Shivaji utilized guerrilla warfare. This involved using small, focused groups with speed, surprise, and superior knowledge of the local terrain to defeat bigger armies.

examples.description: Shivaji used the thick forests of Pratapgad to hide his army and route the veteran Bijapur general Afzal Khan through surprise attacks.

topics.content: Shivaji recognized the need for maritime security to protect coastal resources and control trade. He built coastal forts like Sindhudurg and established a naval force, a revolutionary step as even the Mughals had limited naval presence.
topics.additional_notes: The Navy later successfully challenged European naval supremacy under leaders like Kanhoji Angre.

topics.content: In a daring night raid similar to a modern surgical strike, Shivaji attacked the camp of Mughal nobleman Shaista Khan in Pune. The Khan barely escaped, losing a few fingers, and fled Maharashtra.
topics.additional_notes: This event showed Shivaji's tactical brilliance and ability to strike deep into enemy formations.

topics.content: Shivaji twice sacked Surat, a wealthy Mughal port city, obtainining treasure worth crores. He was careful to spare religious places and charitable houses during these raids.

examples.description: Reports of the sacking of Surat appeared in the London Gazette, one of the earliest English newspapers, signifying Shivaji's growing international fame.

topics.topic_name: Treaty of Purandar and the Agra Escape
topics.content: Against the Rajput general Jai Singh, Shivaji conceded defeat at Purandar for a time. He later visited the court of Aurangzeb in Agra, where he was insulted and put under house arrest.
topics.additional_notes: He escaped by hiding himself and his son Sambhaji in big baskets of sweets being distributed to holy men.

topics.content: In 1674, Shivaji was crowned 'Chhatrapati' with full Vedic rites at Raigad. Like ancient rulers, he started his own era, the Rājyābhiṣheka shaka, to mark this sovereignty.
topics.additional_notes: His title signified he was a 'protector of the people'.

topics.content: Shivaji embarked on a 'conquest of the South', annexing regions in Tamil Nadu and Karnataka. This provided strategic depth that protected the Marathas during later Mughal invasions.

examples.description: During his southern campaign, Shivaji forbade the Dutch from trading Indians as slaves, demonstrating deep concern for his subjects.

topics.content: Shivaji was assisted by the Aṣhṭa Pradhāna Manḍala. Key roles included the Peshwa (Prime Minister), Amatya (Finance), Senapati (Commander-in-Chief), and Nyayadhish (Chief Justice).
topics.additional_notes: Ministers were paid salaries from the state treasury and held non-hereditary posts to ensure centralization of power.

topics.content: Shivaji issued strict instructions to protect subjects. He abolished land assignments and provided pensions to war widows. He even issued orders to protect environmental resources like mango and jackfruit trees.

examples.description: Shivaji's letters stated that trees like mango are like children to the people and should not be cut even for the Navy without explicit permission and alternative wood.

topics.content: Ramachandrapant Amatya described forts as the core of the state. Shivaji built and controlled hundreds of forts to strategically control routes and shelter armies during guerrilla warfare.
topics.additional_notes: Forts allowed the Marathas to survive decades of onslaught by larger empires like the Mughals.

topics.content: The cavalry consisted of Bargirs (horses paid for by the state) and Shiledars (who brought their own horses). In the 18th century, leaders like Mahadji Shinde also raised European-style disciplined infantry.

examples.description: While swords were preferred, the Marathas used guns and metal tube rockets (by 1770) in their campaigns.

topics.content: The Marathas levied taxes from non-Maratha provinces: Chauth (25% of revenue) and Sardeshmukhi (an additional 10%). In return, they provided protection of these areas from external attacks.
topics.additional_notes: Shivaji minted his own coins (Shivrai) in Devanagari script to assert cultural identity.

topics.content: Following the death of Rajaram, Queen Tarabai led the Marathas. Recognizing that the Mughal center was weak, she sent armies into the north, preserving Maratha independence during a critical crisis.
topics.additional_notes: She is credited as the architect of the Maratha's northward expansion.

topics.content: She governed the Holkar state of Indore for 30 years with wisdom and compassion. She is famous for rebuilding hundreds of temples (including Kashi Vishwanath) and promoting the Maheshwar weaving industry.

examples.description: Ahilyabai built ghats, wells, and roads across India, from Kedarnath in the North to Rameswaram in the South.

topics.content: Shivaji's half-brother Ekoji established Maratha rule in Thanjavur (Tamil Nadu). This led to a rich syncretic culture where Marathi, Tamil, and Telugu influences interacted freely.
topics.additional_notes: The Thanjavur Maratha kings were great patrons of arts, poetry, and drama.

topics.content: He was well-versed in many languages and medicine. He established the Dhanwantari Mahal (medicine centre), a printing press, and patronized the early stages of Carnatic music and Bharatanatyam.

examples.description: Serfoji had the history of the Bhonsle family inscribed on the walls of the Brihadishwara temple, one of the largest single inscriptions in India.

topics.content: Under leaders like Bajirao I and Nanasaheb Peshwa, the Maratha Empire expanded across India, briefly controlling areas up to Attock and Lahore (present-day Pakistan).
topics.additional_notes: Despite the disastrous defeat at Panipat in 1761, they recovered under Madhavrao I and Mahadji Shinde.

topics.content: In the 18th and early 19th centuries, the British emerged as chief rivals. Due to internal disunity and British technological superiority, Maratha power ended after the third Anglo-Maratha war (1818).

examples.description: The British effectively took India from the Marathas, who were the largest pan-Indian power before the British conquest.

glossary.definition: A self-governed or sovereign kingdom; the ideal established by Shivaji Maharaj.
The formal title of the sovereign ruler of the Maratha Empire, meaning 'protector of the people'.
A military tactic using speed, surprise, and terrain knowledge to defeat larger armies.
A small hand-held weapon ('tiger's claw') used by Shivaji for close combat.
The council of eight ministers created by Shivaji to assist in various aspects of administration.
A Persian term for 'Prime Minister', who became the de facto head of the Maratha Confederacy in the 18th century.
A tax of 25 per cent of the revenue levied from non-Maratha provinces in exchange for protection.
An additional 10 per cent tax levied on top of Chauth from non-Maratha regions.
Cavalry soldiers whose horses and equipment were provided at state expense.
Cavalry soldiers who provided their own horses and equipment.
A local gathering of officials and prominent men to deliver justice and resolve disputes.
A specialized dictionary commissioned by Shivaji to provide Sanskrit equivalents for Persian administrative terms.
A cursive form of the Devanagari script traditionally used for Maratha correspondence.

summary_points: The Rise of the Marathas was rooted in the cultural consciousness of the Deccan and the spiritual influence of the Bhakti movement.
Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj laid the foundation of Swarajya through tactical brilliance in guerrilla warfare and the establishment of an indigenous Navy.
The Maratha state was governed through the efficient Ashta Pradhan system, prioritizing the welfare of subjects and environmental stewardship.
Under the leadership of the Peshwas, the Marathas became the largest pan-Indian power, reaching from Attock to Thanjavur in the 18th century.
Prominent leaders like Tarabai and Ahilyabai Holkar played crucial roles in expansion, governance, and the restoration of cultural heritage.
The Thanjavur branch of the Marathas contributed significantly to music, medicine, and the early stages of classical dance forms like Bharatanatyam.
Despite their eventual defeat by the British in 1818, the Maratha legacy of self-rule inspired later generations of the Indian freedom movement.
